adolescent_relevance: Blood pressure trajectories can begin in adolescence, and dietary patterns (including sodium exposure), sleep, stress, and activity can shape cohort-level patterns. GENARCH presents hypertension as educational background on population evidence and mechanisms, not as medical advice.

exposure_modifiers.mechanism_hypothesis: Higher sodium intake influences fluid balance and blood pressure regulation pathways in population evidence, with heterogeneity across groups.
Stress-related neuroendocrine signaling can influence vascular tone and inflammatory pathways; causal interpretation varies by study design.
Lower activity levels are associated with adverse cardiometabolic profiles, contributing to elevated blood pressure in population studies.

heritability_estimate.source: Family and GWAS-based estimates (varies by phenotype definition)

genetic_architecture.prs_notes: Hypertension PRS exist in research contexts, but effect estimates and performance vary across ancestries and measurement protocols. GENARCH discusses PRS only as population-level genetic liability and does not compute individual risk.

top_loci.ancestry_composition: European-dominated in many GWAS; broader evidence increasing
top_loci.evidence: RAAS-related genes and regulatory regions are implicated across blood pressure GWAS, supporting endocrine control mechanisms.
Kidney function loci such as UMOD are associated with blood pressure traits in multiple studies.

name: Hypertension

population_equity.data_gaps: Exposure measurement (dietary sodium, stress) can be noisy, and social context can confound associations.
population_equity.gwas_ancestry_breakdown: Large blood pressure GWAS have historically been European-ancestry weighted, though multi-ancestry efforts are expanding.
population_equity.transferability_notes: Effect estimates and PRS performance can vary with ancestry, measurement context, and environmental exposures. GENARCH highlights these limitations and avoids individual-level inference.

references.title: Genome-wide association meta-analysis of blood pressure identifies loci and biological pathways
RAAS pathway regulation and blood pressure control: overview (review)
Dietary sodium and blood pressure: evidence synthesis (review)
Psychosocial stress and health outcomes: mechanisms and evidence (review)
WHO guidelines on physical activity and sedentary behaviour

summary:
Hypertension (high blood pressure) is a common condition defined by persistently elevated arterial pressure. At a biological level, blood pressure reflects interactions among the vasculature, kidneys, endocrine signaling (including the renin–angiotensin–aldosterone system; RAAS), autonomic regulation, and inflammation. Population-level hypertension burden is shaped by diet patterns (including sodium intake), physical activity, psychosocial stressors, and broader environmental and social context.

Genetic epidemiology indicates hypertension is polygenic: many common variants influence blood pressure regulation through kidney function, vascular tone, and hormonal pathways. Individual variants typically have small effects, but aggregate genetic liability can inform population-level mechanism discovery. GENARCH uses this genetic layer for educational mechanistic interpretation—without providing individual risk scores or clinical recommendations. Equity limitations are important: hypertension prevalence and measurement vary by context, and many genetic studies have uneven ancestry representation.
